title: Geometry
objective: Students will explore geometry concepts including Right Angles & Turns, Understanding angles, Parallel and perpendicular lines and more.

steps.content: Have you ever thought about Right Angles & Turns? Let's find out together!
Identify right angles; recognise that two right angles make a half-turn, three make three-quarters, and four make a complete turn
Let's practice together! Use a right-angle checker to identify right angles in shapes and the environment
If you looks at the corner of a piece of paper, can they tell you that's a right angle — and that spinning all the way round takes four right angles?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Right Angles & Turns. Use a right-angle checker to identify right angles in shapes and the environment Classify angles as right angles, less than a right angle, or greater than a right angle
Let's explore Understanding angles! Have you ever wondered about this?
Recognise angles as a property of shape or a description of a turn
Time to try this out: Identify angles at the corners of 2-D shapes
If you ask you to point to an angle on the corner of a book or door frame, can they find it — and explain that swinging a door open is also a kind of turn (angle)?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Understanding angles: Identify angles at the corners of 2-D shapes Describe a turn (e.g. quarter turn, half turn) in terms of the angle made
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Parallel and perpendicular lines!
Identify horizontal and vertical lines and pairs of perpendicular and parallel lines
Let me show you something cool. Point out horizontal and vertical lines in shapes and real-world contexts
If you looks at a set of railway tracks or a window frame, can they point to which lines are parallel (never crossing) and which are perpendicular (meeting at a right angle)?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Parallel and perpendicular lines. Point out horizontal and vertical lines in shapes and real-world contexts Identify a pair of parallel lines (lines that never meet and are always the same distance apart)
Let's discover the amazing world of Angles in triangles (age 7+)!
Recognise and draw shapes having specified attributes (e.g. a given number of angles or equal faces); identify triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and cubes
Time to try this out: Draw a shape with exactly 5 sides (pentagon)
If you is given instructions like "draw a shape with 5 sides" or "draw a shape with all equal angles," can they draw it correctly?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Angles in triangles (age 7+). Draw a shape with exactly 5 sides (pentagon) Identify all quadrilaterals in a set of mixed shapes
Let's explore 2-D shapes (age 7+)! Have you ever wondered about this?
Draw 2-D shapes and make 3-D shapes using modelling materials; recognise 3-D shapes in different orientations and describe them
Time to try this out: Draw a triangle, rectangle, pentagon, or hexagon accurately
If you is given a cube to look at, can they draw it on paper — and then make a cube-shaped model from modelling clay or construction materials?

You did it! Here's what we explored about 2-D shapes (age 7+): Draw a triangle, rectangle, pentagon, or hexagon accurately Construct a cube or cuboid from modelling materials (e.g. straws and connectors)
